content.full_text:
Office of City Clerk                                                1017 Middlefield Road
                                                                              P.O. Box 391
                                                                              Redwood City, California 94064
                                                                              Telephone (415) 780-7220
                                                                              FAX (415) 780-7225


July 3, 1992


Fair Political Practices Commission
Technical Assistance and Analysis Division
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814-0807

Subject:  Newspaper Article regarding Dan Levin's campaigns

Dear Marianne:

On July 9, 1992 the enclosed article appeared in the San Mateo Times.
As far as I know this is the first time Mr. Levin has asked for
contributions.  Do I, as City Clerk of Redwood City, have any further
obligations to contact Mr. Levin again and advise him he is in
violation of the Fair Political Practices Commission's filing
requirements?

Very truly yours,

Arlyn C. Jorgenson
Arlyn C. Jorgenson
City Clerk

Enclosures

The image appears to be almost entirely blank or white, with only some very faint marks or artifacts visible in the upper right corner that are too unclear to transcribe with confidence. The rest of the page is essentially empty.

# Peninsula

"Pear-root Boil" at RC counter tunes up (yet

By JAMES PETERSON

A Developer asks residents for list choice on its plots

[IMAGE: Black and white photograph showing what appears to be a construction or development site]

SF eye plant to modernize Orange Park

Would spend $6 million to upgrade facility

By MASTHEAD EDITION

[The rest of the document contains dense newspaper text that is largely illegible in this scan quality. The main visible headlines and article beginnings are transcribed above, but the body text is too blurry and degraded to accurately transcribe without extensive use of [illegible] markers. The document appears to be a vintage newspaper page with multiple columns and articles discussing local development and planning issues.]

I can see this is a very faint or mostly blank document image. The image appears to be predominantly white/blank with only some very faint marks or artifacts visible in the upper right corner and lower portion, but no clearly legible text that I can reliably transcribe.

[illegible]

embedding.qa_text: July 3, 1992 Fair Political Practices Commission Technical Assistance and Analysis Division 428 J Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, CA 95814-0807 Subject: Newspaper Article regarding Dan Levin’s campaigns Dear Marianne: On July 9, 1992 the enclosed article appeared in the San Mateo Times. As far as I know this is the first time Mr. Levin has asked for contributions. Do I, as City Clerk of Redwood City, have any further obligations to contact Mr. Levin again and advise him he is in violation of the Fair Political Practices Commission’s filing requirements? Very truly yours, Arlyn C. Jorgenson City Clerk Enclosures The image you provided is mostly blank, with very little visible content. There are some faint marks or smudges in the upper right corner, but no clear text or significant features to describe. If you have another page or section of the document that you would like me to analyze, please provide it. The article titled "Fear not: BFI bill not going up — yet" discusses the efforts of Dan Levin, a developer organizing what he calls a citizens movement to get new people on the City Council. Levin has taken an unusual approach of asking voters who they want to see run. Levin and five other potential candidates are featured in a mailing sent to registered voters. Developer asks residents to list choices By KAREN PEITERRSON Times Staff Writer REDWOOD CITY — Dan Levin, the developer organizing what he calls a citizens movement to get new people on the City Council, has taken the unusual approach of asking voters who they want to see run. The campaign, he said, will focus on the "general ineptitude" of the existing council and concentrate on three areas of concern: high-quality housing, downtown revitalization and putting the city's port to better use. Levin and his group, Redwood City Citizens for Recall, want to develop the waterfront. More. Relax. The bottom line always, just the format of the bill from Browning. Ferris Industries has changed. The format change has been brought about so that people can see what they're paying for, said Liz Diamond of the Public Works Department. "Some folks here at the city felt it appropriate for people to understand what they're paying for — that it's not just garbage, that other facets are involved," Diamond said. "We asked BFI to modify the bill and they cooperated." SSF eyes plan to modernize Orange Park Would spend $6 million to upgrade facility By MARSHALL WILSON Times Staff Writer SOUTH SAN FRANCISCO — A $5 million plan to expand and renovate Orange Memorial Park with new play fields, more parking and upgraded buildings is faced with charges that he was trying to buy his way onto the council — along with a coalition that would support his positions — Levin backed off. Recently, he failed to win approval for a downtown condominium project. "This is all new to me," Levin said Wednesday of his first bid for public office. "But I think we never thought
